title: The Feed-Forward Limit
concept: Standard neural networks assume inputs are independent and fixed in size, making them unable to process variable-length text sequences or track context.

exercises.prompt: Why do standard neural networks struggle to read long paragraphs?

options.text: They assume inputs are independent and fixed in size.
They automatically understand human grammar rules.

exercises.explain: Traditional feed-forward networks process inputs in one direction and assume all inputs are independent, making them struggle with sequences like text.

cards.title: Reading Without a Memory
cards.body: Have you ever tried to understand a movie by looking at random, disconnected screenshots? Probably not! To get the story, you need to watch the frames in order and remember what happened in the previous scene. Traditional neural networks can't do this—they look at each input in total isolation!

cards.title: What is the Feed-Forward Limit?
cards.body: A feed-forward network is a basic neural network where information moves in only one direction—forward. It assumes that each input (like a single word) is completely independent of the others and that the input size never changes. This makes it impossible for the network to understand the flow of a story or the order of words in a sentence.

cards.title: The Memoryless Reader
cards.body: Imagine reading a book, but every time you turn the page, your brain is completely wiped clean! You forget the characters, the plot, and the sentence you just finished. That is how a standard feed-forward network sees the world. It processes each word as a standalone object, completely unaware of what came before it.

cards.title: Struggling with a Simple Sentence
cards.body: If you feed the sentence "I love coding" into a standard feed-forward network, it treats "I", "love", and "coding" as isolated islands. It doesn't link them together to understand that "I" is the one doing the "loving". If you suddenly add a fourth word, "highly", the network will break because it was only built to expect exactly three inputs.

cards.title: Size Doesn't Solve It
cards.body: Some people think that if you make a standard neural network larger, it will eventually understand text sequences. But size isn't the issue! No matter how big a standard network is, its structure prevents it from remembering past inputs or handling sentences of different lengths. You need a different architecture altogether.

cards.title: Memory vs. Memoryless
cards.body: See how traditional networks compare to what we actually need for understanding language and sequences.

right.text: Flexible input size. Links words together to keep track of context and order.

left.text: Fixed input size. Treats words as disconnected, independent blocks with no memory.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of standard feed-forward networks when dealing with text?

options.text: They process information too quickly for humans to read.
They cannot track context or handle inputs of different sizes.
They only work with images and cannot read numbers at all.
They require constant internet connection to remember words.

exercises.explain: Standard feed-forward networks process each input in isolation, which prevents them from keeping track of the order of words or the context of a paragraph.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A standard feed-forward network treats the third word in a sentence without knowing what the first two words were.
exercises.explain: This is true. Because feed-forward networks have no memory mechanism, they treat every input as a brand new, isolated event.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how standard networks view incoming data:
exercises.sentence: Standard networks treat inputs as independent, which makes them fail at tracking context.
exercises.explain: Standard feed-forward networks assume that each input is completely independent of the ones before it, meaning they cannot track context or history.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about feed-forward networks is AI-generated and false?

options.text: Feed-forward networks assume that one word in a sentence has no relationship to the next word.
Feed-forward networks easily stretch their input layers to accommodate sentences of any length.
Because they lack memory loops, feed-forward networks process everything in a single forward pass.

exercises.explain: The second option is created by an AI trying to guess how networks work. Standard feed-forward networks cannot dynamically resize their structure to fit sentences; they require rigid, fixed-size inputs.

exercises.prompt: Why do standard feed-forward networks struggle when you give them sentences of different lengths?

options.text: They automatically loop back to read the text multiple times.
They dynamically grow new brain cells for longer sentences.
They require a rigid, fixed input size and cannot naturally resize.
They ignore all grammar rules to save computational power.

exercises.explain: Standard feed-forward networks require a fixed input size because their weight connections are set in stone. They cannot stretch to accommodate longer sentences, nor can they shrink for shorter ones without awkward padding.
